   i don't have a kiln or any way of baking the bricks, so would be reliant on the sun. i was thinking maybe adding water and maybe some loam?    aside: there are no suitable tags for my question and i do not have the reputation to add any, i'm thinking i could be on the wrong site for this question, if i am, please advise me of a more suitable one. i have recently bought a medium sized plot of wood/scrub land. half of this plot was once used to dump shale from coal mines, which means i have a lot of shale.    i have been wondering/looking for possible uses for the stuff. one thought i've had is to make "mud bricks" however i can't find any mention of whether it is possible or not. i know the traditional method uses loamy soil but would it work with shale?    i don't have a kiln or any way of baking the bricks, so would be reliant on the sun. i was thinking maybe adding water and maybe some loam?    aside: there are no suitable tags for my question and i do not have the reputation to add any, i'm thinking i could be on the wrong site for this question, if i am, please advise me of a more suitable one. shale, which is a fine-grained sedimentary rock formed from compacted mud, is quite different in its properties from the loamy soils traditionally used for making sun-dried mud bricks (adobe). loam is ideal for brick-making because it has a balanced mix of sand, silt, and clay, which allows it to hold together when shaped and dried. shale, on the other hand, tends to break down into flaky, plate-like fragments rather than forming a cohesive mass. if you try to make mud bricks using only crushed or powdery shale, the bricks may not bind together well and could crumble easily after drying

i was wondering if we could possibly reduce the severity of, or steer a hurricane by changing albedo (e.g. dying the part ocean with a temporary light colored dye)? my thought would be to put this dye well in front of the path of a hurricane, with enough time to reflect the sunlight back off the ocean and cool the ocean surface. when the hurricane arrives there, the ocean surface should then be cooler, leading to the hurricane being less powerful. i am also wondering if this could be used to steer the hurricane somehow (e.g. if a hurricane would be drawn to or repelled away from cool ocean surface). if so, could we use that to steer a hurricane away from populated areas?    some potential methods (there may be others):    use light colored dye injected into the ocean    use light colored smoke to block the sun from reaching the ocean    possibly use "cloud seeding" ahead of the hurricane and form clouds to block the sun light from warming the ocean. - covered in the alternate question    perhaps a "surface film" could also be applied temporarily to part of the ocean (sort of like a "light colored oil spill", hopefully with something safer for the environment than oil). - covered in the alternate question    if this experiment were to be conducted, i would think it should be done far from inhabited land. environmental impacts would need to be thought out too, e.g. would a film block the carbon dioxide / oxygen exchange in that area and cause severe environmental impact? researchers studying the possibility of life on mars, including the potential for present or past multicellular life, focus on several key traces, markers, and martian environments that could support this hypothesis. signs they look for include preserved biosignatures such as fossilized cell structures, patterns of layered sedimentary rocks that could indicate ancient microbial mats, and the presence of complex organic molecules. additional markers might involve certain isotopic ratios, like those of carbon or sulfur, that often result from biological activity on earth. environments of particular interest are those where liquid water existed or still exists—for example, ancient lakebeds, dried-up river document 1 - discusses nasa's perseverance mars rover's search for ancient life, signs that mars' jezero crater was once a lake, and the strategy to collect samples that might preserve signs of life. - mentions clay minerals carried by water into jezero crater. - details that the perseverance rover seeks signs of past microbial life and collects samples of rock and regolith. document 5 - mentions that the past flow of liquid water on mars demonstrates its potential for habitability. - notes challenges to sustaining organic life on

is there a package for r to plot schmidt nets, like it is done in geology?    so far, i only know software like stereonet. however i think it would make a lot of sense to handle these data in r, to have so much more possibilities in calculations and statistics. is there a package for r to plot schmidt nets, like it is done in geology?    so far, i only know software like stereonet. however i think it would make a lot of sense to handle these data in r, to have so much more possibilities in calculations and statistics. yes, there are r packages available for plotting schmidt nets (stereonets), which are commonly used in geology to display the orientation of planes and lines, such as bedding, faults, and fold axes. one of the most popular packages is **rfoc**. the rfoc package allows users to plot lower-hemisphere equal-area (schmidt) and equal-angle (wulff) stereonets, and provides functions for plotting planes, lines, and performing basic structural calculations.

another useful package is **geomorph**, which also contains some functions for plotting orientations on stereonets, though its primary focus is schmidt net at the far right of the net. the two sectors have the same area on the sphere and the same area on the disk. the angle-distorting property can be seen by examining the grid lines; most of them do not intersect at right angles on the schmidt net.

generally speaking, iron is considered to be a very common resource. but i cannot find any evidence that there is any of it at all in new guinea. copper and gold, yes. even nickel and cobalt. iron on the nearby island of bougainville. but no mention of iron.    are there really no useful iron deposits on the entire island? or is it more like, there are some, just not big enough to make the news? generally speaking, iron is considered to be a very common resource. but i cannot find any evidence that there is any of it at all in new guinea. copper and gold, yes. even nickel and cobalt. iron on the nearby island of bougainville. but no mention of iron.    are there really no useful iron deposits on the entire island? or is it more like, there are some, just not big enough to make the news? iron is indeed one of the most abundant elements on earth, and significant iron ore deposits are found in many regions around the world. however, when it comes to new guinea, the situation is somewhat unusual. while new guinea is rich in other mineral resources such as copper, gold, nickel, and cobalt—which have attracted significant mining investments—there is little to no evidence of major iron ore deposits on the island. geological surveys and mining records confirm the presence of large-scale iron ore on nearby islands, such as bougainville, but not in new guinea itself.

that said, the absence of newsworthy large iron ore deposits does not necessarily mean gold was first discovered in papua new guinea in 1852 as accidental traces in pottery from redscar bay on the papuan peninsula.

what events took place in our solar system which led to formation of the earth? i have heard from sources that a big giant star exploded and the earth was formed. is this true ? what events took place in our solar system which led to formation of the earth? i have heard from sources that a big giant star exploded and the earth was formed. is this true ? passage:  
the formation of the earth is closely linked to the origin of our solar system, which began about 4.6 billion years ago. scientists believe the process started with a massive cloud of gas and dust in space, known as a solar nebula. this nebula was likely enriched with heavier elements by earlier generations of stars, including material from exploding stars called supernovae. the shockwaves from one or more nearby supernova explosions may have triggered the collapse of the nebula under its own gravity.

seems like the earth day related crowd sourcing project to create the first global soundscape by encouraging smartphone owners around the world to download and use an app developed to record the sounds around them is interesting, though it's unclear what scientific value it would have. is there any scientific value to understanding soundscapes on a global basis? seems like the earth day related crowd sourcing project to create the first global soundscape by encouraging smartphone owners around the world to download and use an app developed to record the sounds around them is interesting, though it's unclear what scientific value it would have. is there any scientific value to understanding soundscapes on a global basis? yes, there is significant scientific value in understanding soundscapes on a global basis. soundscapes—the collection of sounds that characterize a given environment—provide important information about both natural and human-influenced habitats. by crowdsourcing audio recordings from around the world, scientists can gain a comprehensive picture of acoustic environments across different regions, seasons, and times of day. this data can help researchers monitor biodiversity, track changes in ecosystems, and assess the impact of human activities such as urbanization and noise pollution. for example, the presence or absence of certain animal calls can be used to infer species distribution and abundance. moreover, long-term monitoring of global soundscapes soundscape ecology is the study of the acoustic relationships between living organisms, human and other, and their environment, whether the organisms are marine or terrestrial.

what percentage of earth's surface is arid?is there available information on how much of earth surface is arid climate? what percentage of earth's surface is arid?is there available information on how much of earth surface is arid climate? arid regions, which are characterized by extremely low rainfall and limited vegetation, make up a significant portion of earth's surface. according to scientific estimates, about 30% of the earth's land surface is classified as arid or semi-arid. when considering the entire surface of the earth—including both land and oceans—arid regions account for approximately 10-12% of the total surface area. these areas are predominantly found in deserts such as the sahara, arabian, gobi, and parts of australia, where the climate is typically dry, hot, and receives less than 250 millimeters (10 inches) of rainfall annually. this information climate classification systems such as the köppen climate classification system use average annual rainfall to help differentiate between differing climate regimes. most of australia is semi-arid or desert... group b: arid (desert and semi-arid) climates

what magnitude does it feel like when far away in an earthquake? i can't find a good way to say this but, if i had some info about an earthquake (i.e. magnitude 8, originating in water) and if i had a distance (i.e. 850 miles/ 1370 km) could i calculate what it would be if that specific location was the epicenter? so, maybe it would be like a 3 or 4 maybe?i'm looking for a formula or calculator. if it is not possible, please tell me! it is fine if it is rough. what magnitude does it feel like when far away in an earthquake? i can't find a good way to say this but, if i had some info about an earthquake (i.e. magnitude 8, originating in water) and if i had a distance (i.e. 850 miles/ 1370 km) could i calculate what it would be if that specific location was the epicenter? so, maybe it would be like a 3 or 4 maybe?i'm looking for a formula or calculator. if it is not possible, please tell me! it is fine if it is rough. when you are far away from the epicenter of an earthquake, the shaking you feel is generally much weaker than at the source. the original earthquake is measured with a magnitude (such as 8.0), but as seismic waves travel through the earth, their energy is spread out and absorbed, making the shaking less intense with distance. the intensity you feel at a specific location is influenced by several factors: the earthquake’s magnitude, your distance from the epicenter, the depth of the earthquake, and the type of ground between the epicenter and your location.

to estimate how strong the earthquake feels at your location, scientists use what is called the richter magnitude of an earthquake is determined from the logarithm of the amplitude of waves recorded by seismographs. adjustments are included to compensate for the variation in the distance between the various seismographs and the epicenter of the earthquake.

for some time now, i've noticed that mountains in southeast asia seem to be unusually "jagged" with steep surfaces. my basic knowledge of how mountains are formed (via geologic processes) tells me that it's more common to find mountains like these in glacial areas (e.g. northern canada, norway, etc.), whereas those formed by tectonic plate collisions appear more "traditional" (e.g. main range of himalayas, andes, etc.), and geologically old mountains have a weathered appearance (e.g. appalachians).    what caused/causes these se asian mountains to have their distinct appearance? for some time now, i've noticed that mountains in southeast asia seem to be unusually "jagged" with steep surfaces. my basic knowledge of how mountains are formed (via geologic processes) tells me that it's more common to find mountains like these in glacial areas (e.g. northern canada, norway, etc.), whereas those formed by tectonic plate collisions appear more "traditional" (e.g. main range of himalayas, andes, etc.), and geologically old mountains have a weathered appearance (e.g. appalachians).    what caused/causes these se asian mountains to have their distinct appearance? the distinct “jagged” and steep appearance of many mountains in southeast asia is primarily a result of their underlying geology and climatic conditions rather than glacial activity, which is rare in this region. many of these mountains are composed of limestone, formed from ancient marine sediments. over millions of years, tectonic movements uplifted these limestone beds, creating rugged mountain ranges. once exposed at the surface, southeast asia’s warm, humid, and rainy tropical climate accelerates chemical weathering and erosion. limestone is especially susceptible to dissolution by slightly acidic rainwater, a process known as karstification. 

as rainwater seeps into cracks and at zones of ocean-to-ocean subduction a deep trench to forms in an arc shape. the upper mantle of the subducted plate then heats and magma rises to form curving chains of volcanic islands e.g. the aleutian islands, the mariana islands, the japanese island arc. at zones of ocean-to-continent subduction mountain ranges form, e.g. the andes, the cascade range.

i have a theoretical exercise where i calculated the amount of co2 that is released to the atmosphere per year (4.6 ppm). we assume that the relative increase of co2 is 2 ppm, hence the percentage of co2 that stays in the atmosphere is around 2/4.6 ~ 40 %.    what is the technical term for this percentage? i have a theoretical exercise where i calculated the amount of co2 that is released to the atmosphere per year (4.6 ppm). we assume that the relative increase of co2 is 2 ppm, hence the percentage of co2 that stays in the atmosphere is around 2/4.6 ~ 40 %.    what is the technical term for this percentage? passage:  
the percentage of emitted carbon dioxide (co₂) that remains in the atmosphere each year, as calculated by dividing the observed annual atmospheric increase by the total anthropogenic emissions, is referred to as the "airborne fraction." the airborne fraction represents the proportion of co₂ emissions from human activities (such as fossil fuel combustion and deforestation) that is not absorbed by natural sinks like the oceans and terrestrial biosphere, but instead accumulates in the atmosphere. in your example, with 4.6 ppm of co₂ released annually and a relative increase of 2 ppm, the airborne fraction is approximately 40%. this concept emissions have been caused by burning fossil fuels, and a little less than half of that has stayed in the atmosphere. since the start of the industrial revolution, atmospheric co2 concentration have been increasing, causing global warming and ocean acidification.

reviewing market regulatory standards i came across the problem of finding a source that i could cite of concise, worldwide accepted industry geophysical terms.    so far i know schlumberger oilfield glossary, seg wiki, spe petrowiki that give some information on some terms. in practice i need something like this definition in seg wiki for seismic crooked lines. although in this case i don't think the term is well defined.    i am looking for a worldwide industry accepted glossary that i could rely on and cite. the broader it is the best will be for having less problems in future. where would be the best place to find such source of information? any other suggestions will be welcome. reviewing market regulatory standards i came across the problem of finding a source that i could cite of concise, worldwide accepted industry geophysical terms.    so far i know schlumberger oilfield glossary, seg wiki, spe petrowiki that give some information on some terms. in practice i need something like this definition in seg wiki for seismic crooked lines. although in this case i don't think the term is well defined.    i am looking for a worldwide industry accepted glossary that i could rely on and cite. the broader it is the best will be for having less problems in future. where would be the best place to find such source of information? any other suggestions will be welcome. finding a single, universally accepted glossary for geophysical and oilfield industry terms can indeed be challenging, as many sources tend to be either regional, proprietary, or specific to a certain subfield. while the schlumberger oilfield glossary, seg wiki, and spe petrowiki are well-established and widely used, they each come with some limitations regarding comprehensiveness, authoritative consensus on definitions, and ease of citation for regulatory or standards purposes.

for the most universally recognized and citable glossary, the **society of exploration geophysicists (seg)** and the **society of petroleum engineers (spe)** are generally considered in february 2017, a group of volunteers started translating robert sheriff's encyclopedic dictionary into spanish. the fourth edition of seg's bestseller encyclopedic dictionary was last updated in 2002. it's a valuable reference that now needs to be updated for the next generation of scientists. the seg wiki committee and seg staff seek volunteers with geophysical knowledge to update robert sheriff's encyclopedic dictionary on the seg wiki.

why is the sea level in hudson bay decreasing so much? hudson bay is pretty far up north, much closer to glaciers. would it make sense for it to recede at this level with sources of fresh water relatively close? why is the sea level in hudson bay decreasing so much? hudson bay is pretty far up north, much closer to glaciers. would it make sense for it to recede at this level with sources of fresh water relatively close? hudson bay’s sea level is decreasing primarily due to a process called **post-glacial rebound** (also known as glacial isostatic adjustment). during the last ice age, massive glaciers covered the region, pressing down on the earth’s crust. when these glaciers melted thousands of years ago, the immense weight was lifted, and the land beneath began to slowly rise, or "rebound." this rising land causes the *local* sea level to fall relative to the shoreline, even though global sea levels are rising due to climate change and melting ice.

although hudson bay is relatively close to current sources of fresh water, such as the sea level rise is not uniform around the globe. some land masses are moving up or down as a consequence of subsidence (land sinking or settling) or post-glacial rebound (land rising as melting ice reduces weight). therefore, local relative sea level rise may be higher or lower than the global average. on the other hand, relative sea level around the hudson bay in canada and the northern baltic is falling due to post-glacial isostatic rebound.

are clouds a solid, liquid, or gas?  i have been looking online and they are often described ambiguously as a "mass". for instance, from nasa:  a cloud is a mass of water drops or ice crystals suspended in the atmosphere. clouds form when water condenses in the sky. the condensation lets us see the water vapor. there are many different types of clouds. clouds are an important part of earth’s weather and climate.  since they describe it as a vapor, that makes me think it is indeed a gas. but condensation is by definition the change of state from a gas to a liquid. so that makes it sound like it could be a liquid, or contain liquid droplets rather than "freely moving" molecules that typically make up a gas.  but at another web site, which doesn't seem all that authoritative, it says that clouds are a solid, liquid, and gas simultaneously!  a cloud is a liquid, a solid, and a gas.  that does seem intriguing. if i remember correctly, liquids are supposed to be incompressible, and clouds are likely compressible. are clouds a solid, liquid, or gas? what causes the typical red, pink or orange colors seen in sun-rises and sun-sets?  how come the sky doesn't just turn a darker blue? what causes the typical red, pink or orange colors seen in sun-rises and sun-sets?  how come the sky doesn't just turn a darker blue? the typical red, pink, or orange colors seen in sunrises and sunsets are caused by a phenomenon called scattering. during sunrise and sunset, the sun is low on the horizon and its light has to travel through a greater thickness of the earth’s atmosphere compared to when it is overhead. as sunlight passes through this thicker layer of air, tiny particles and molecules in the atmosphere scatter the shorter wavelengths of light (blue and violet) more strongly than the longer wavelengths (red, orange, and yellow). because the blue and violet light gets scattered out of our line of sight, the remaining light we see is dominated by the reds, pinks as a ray of white sunlight travels through the atmosphere to an observer, some of the colors are scattered out of the beam by air molecules and airborne particles, changing the final color of the beam the viewer sees. because the shorter wavelength components, such as blue and green, scatter more strongly, these colors are preferentially removed from the beam.[10]

the "degree" (often denoted as n) indicates the level of spatial detail captured by the model. each increase in degree allows the model to represent smaller and finer features of the gravitational field, such as local variations due to mountain ranges or ocean trenches. the "order" (often denoted as m the modes of oscillations are specified by three numbers, e.g., nslm, where l is the angular order number (or spherical harmonic degree, see spherical harmonics for more details). the number m is the azimuthal order number. it may take on 2l+1 values from −l to +l. the number n is the radial order number.

this trapped layer can hold smog, smoke, and other pollutants near the surface, sometimes for days. as a result, air quality can decline significantly, especially in cities or valleys where inversions are common. in meteorology, an inversion (or 'temperature inversion') is a layer of warmer air held above colder air. normally, air temperature gradually decreases as altitude increases, but in an inversion this relationship is reversed. an inversion traps air pollution, such as smog, close to the ground. an inversion can also suppress convection by acting as a 'cap'. temperature inversion can cause freezing rain in cold climates.

meteorologists define the seasons differently from astronomers primarily for practical and statistical reasons. in meteorology, the seasons are based on the annual temperature cycle and follow the calendar months: winter runs from december 1 to the end of february, spring march 1 to may 31, summer june 1 to august 31, and autumn september 1 to november 30. this approach allows for easier comparison and analysis of climate data, as each "meteorological season" consists of whole months and always includes the same dates each year.

beginning the meteorological seasons at the start of the month, rather than matching up with the the astronomical seasons are based on the position of earth in relation to the sun, whereas the meteorological seasons are based on the annual temperature cycle.

in the mainstream media the problem of ocean level rise is presented rather... over-simplistically - ice melts -> more water flows into the oceans -> water level rises -> continents sink.  but it is in reality a complex system, it is not like pouring water in a glass... or a lake that resides on a continental plate.  both continental and oceanic plates float on top of the mantle as both are lighter and lower density, and both exert weight and compress the mantle.  therefore, if water is removed from continental plates by means of melting ice, the continental plate gets lighter. and when that water migrates to the ocean, the oceanic plate gets heavier. and since for every action there is equal in force and opposite in direction reaction, it seems logical that the now heavier oceanic plate will actually push the now lighter continental plate up, to some extent compensating for or possibly even reversing the change in ocean level relative to the continental plate.  so, do the estimates account for the dynamic properties of that complex system? in popular media, ocean level rise is often depicted in a straightforward way: ice melts, extra water pours into the ocean, and the sea simply rises, overwhelming coastlines. however, the reality is far more complex due to the dynamic nature of earth's crust and the mantle beneath. both continental and oceanic plates "float" atop the viscous mantle, pressing down with their mass—whether that mass is rock, water, or ice.
